title: REST APIs & HTTP Fundamentals
user_familiarity: You've used Python's requests library to call APIs. You haven't built a REST server before.
relevance_to_project: REST is the communication protocol between the WellBegun Svelte frontend and the FastAPI backend. Every button click, form submission, and data fetch goes through REST endpoints defined in src/wellbegun/routers/.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content_md:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is the architectural style your WellBegun app uses for the frontend (Svelte) to communicate with the backend (FastAPI). Think of it as a standardized contract: the frontend sends HTTP requests to specific URLs, and the backend responds with JSON data.

If you've used Python's `requests` library to call APIs, you've already used REST — you just may not have built the server side before.

key_takeaways: REST APIs use standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to perform CRUD operations
The server is stateless — each request contains all the info needed to process it
JSON is the lingua franca for data exchange between frontend and backend

sections.title: Core Concepts
sections.content_md:
### HTTP Methods Map to CRUD Operations

In Python, you might write functions like `get_collection()`, `create_collection()`, `delete_collection()`. REST maps these to HTTP methods:

| HTTP Method | CRUD Operation | Example |
|------------|----------------|--------|
| GET | Read | Fetch all collections |
| POST | Create | Create a new collection |
| PUT | Update | Modify collection title |
| DELETE | Delete | Remove a collection |

### URLs as Resource Identifiers

REST URLs describe *what* you're working with, not *what you're doing*. Instead of `/api/deleteCollection?id=5`, REST uses `DELETE /api/collections/5`.

WellBegun's URL structure follows this convention:
- `/api/collections/` — the collection list (GET = list all, POST = create)
- `/api/collections/5` — a specific collection (GET = fetch, PUT = update, DELETE = remove)
- `/api/collections/5/items` — items within collection 5 (POST = add item)
- `/api/collections/5/activate` — lifecycle action on collection 5

### Request and Response Bodies

When creating or updating resources, data travels as JSON in the request body. When reading, the server returns JSON in the response body. The `Content-Type: application/json` header tells the server "this request body is JSON".

### HTTP Status Codes

Status codes tell the client what happened:
- **200** OK — Success (used for GET, PUT)
- **201** Created — Resource created (used for POST)
- **404** Not Found — Resource doesn't exist
- **422** Unprocessable Entity — Validation error

Notice FastAPI's `status_code=201` — it returns 201 for successful creation instead of the default 200.

### Statelessness

Each HTTP request is self-contained. The server doesn't remember previous requests. In WellBegun, every request includes all the context it needs — the collection ID is in the URL, the data is in the body, and the database session is freshly created via `Depends(get_db)`.

key_takeaways: HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) map directly to CRUD operations (Read, Create, Update, Delete)
REST URLs identify resources, not actions — use DELETE /api/collections/5 instead of /api/deleteCollection?id=5
JSON request and response bodies carry data, with Content-Type headers signaling the format
HTTP status codes communicate outcomes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 404 Not Found, 422 Validation Error
Statelessness means each request is self-contained — no server-side session memory between requests

sections.title: In This Project
sections.content_md:
WellBegun's REST API lives in `src/wellbegun/routers/` with one file per resource type. The main app at `src/wellbegun/main.py` wires them together.

Each router handles a resource type. The `prefix="/api"` means all routes start with `/api/`. Combined with the router's own prefix (`prefix="/collections"`), the full URL becomes `/api/collections/`.

The frontend mirrors this structure in `frontend/src/lib/api/`, where each file contains functions that call the corresponding backend endpoints.

code_examples.annotation: A GET endpoint that lists all collections. The router prefix /collections plus the app prefix /api makes the full URL /api/collections/.
The frontend counterpart — calls GET /api/collections/ and parses the JSON response into a typed array.

code_examples.code:
app.include_router(health.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(tags.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(projects.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(logs.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(notes.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(sources.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(actors.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(activities.router, prefix="/api")

app.include_router(plans.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(search.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(active_context.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(assistant.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(scaffolding.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(knowledge.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(web_proxy.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(journal.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(coffee.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(categories.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(collections.router, prefix="/api")
app.include_router(documents.router)
app.include_router(workspaces.router, prefix="/api")
code_examples.annotation: The main FastAPI app wires together all routers under the /api prefix. Each router adds its own resource prefix (e.g., /collections), forming URLs like /api/collections/.

sections.title: Guided Examples
sections.content_md:
### Example 1: Full CRUD Lifecycle

Here's the complete lifecycle of a collection in WellBegun, showing the REST pattern end-to-end: CREATE with POST, READ with GET, UPDATE with PUT, and DELETE with DELETE.

### Example 2: Error Handling with Status Codes

The service layer raises `ValueError` for business logic errors (e.g., invalid status). The router catches it and converts it to a 422 HTTP response. The frontend checks `res.ok` and extracts the error detail from the JSON response body.

code_examples.annotation: CREATE — POST /api/collections/ returns 201 on success with the new resource as JSON.
READ — GET /api/collections/{id} returns the resource or 404 if not found.
UPDATE — PUT /api/collections/{id} applies partial updates and returns the modified resource.
DELETE — DELETE /api/collections/{id} removes the resource and returns a simple confirmation.
Error handling — ValueError from the service layer is caught and converted to a 422 HTTP response with a descriptive detail message.
Frontend error handling — checks res.ok, extracts the detail field from the error JSON, and throws a descriptive Error.
Frontend CREATE — sends POST with JSON body and Content-Type header, then parses the response as the created Collection.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.content_md:
### Exercise 1: Trace a Request

Open `frontend/src/lib/api/collections.ts` and `src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py` side by side. For the `deleteCollection` function, trace the full request path: What URL does the frontend call? What HTTP method? What does the backend return?

### Exercise 2: Spot the Pattern

Look at the lifecycle endpoints in `src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py` (lines 61-90). These use `POST` instead of `PUT`. Why might `POST /collections/5/activate` be used instead of `PUT /collections/5` with `{is_active: true}` in the body?

exercises.task: Open frontend/src/lib/api/collections.ts and src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py side by side. For the deleteCollection function, trace the full request path: What URL does the frontend call? What HTTP method? What does the backend return?
hints: Look at lines 34-36 in the frontend file and lines 52-56 in the router file.
The frontend uses fetch with method: 'DELETE'.

exercises.task: Look at the lifecycle endpoints in src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py (lines 61-90). These use POST instead of PUT. Why might POST /collections/5/activate be used instead of PUT /collections/5 with {is_active: true} in the body?
hints: Actions like 'activate' and 'archive' are state transitions with side effects.
Archiving also deactivates — requiring the client to know which fields to update together would be error-prone.
Using a dedicated POST endpoint makes the intent explicit and lets the server handle the logic.

sections.title: Knowledge Check
sections.content_md: Test your understanding of REST APIs and HTTP fundamentals as used in the WellBegun project.

questions.question: Which HTTP method should you use to create a new resource?
questions.explanation: POST is used to create new resources. In WellBegun, POST /api/collections/ creates a new collection (src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py:31).

questions.question: What does a 404 status code mean?
options: The request body had invalid data
The requested resource was not found
The server encountered an internal error
questions.explanation: 404 means the requested resource was not found. In WellBegun, the routers raise HTTPException(status_code=404) when a collection or item doesn't exist in the database (src/wellbegun/routers/collections.py:27).

questions.question: Why does DELETE /api/collections/5 include the ID in the URL rather than in the request body?
options: DELETE requests can't have a body
REST convention: the URL identifies the resource being acted upon
It's faster for the server to parse
The frontend doesn't support request bodies
questions.explanation: In REST, the URL path identifies the resource. The HTTP method says what to do with it. This makes the API self-documenting: DELETE /api/collections/5 clearly means 'delete collection 5'.

questions.question: In WellBegun, what header must be included when sending JSON data in a request body?
questions.explanation: Content-Type: application/json tells the server the request body is JSON. You can see it in frontend/src/lib/api/collections.ts:19 where POST and PUT requests include headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.content_md: Curated resources for deepening your understanding of REST APIs and HTTP.
